context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda stuck on this reflection abt “development as freedom” and rights-based planning, and idk where to start. can u just explain the whole thing in a simple way first?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you get unstuck! But instead of me explaining it, let's start with what you already think.

When you hear the phrase 'development as freedom,' what's your gut reaction? What do you think it might mean for a country or a person to 'develop'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole concept to them, which would essentially mean giving them the answer. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge and get them thinking. I'll ask a simple opening question to help them engage with the core idea on their own terms, without scaffolding too much at the start.

exchanges.student: ngl my brain just says “more choices maybe?” but also that feels way too vague. that didn’t narrow it much, so can u give me the big-picture version first?
exchanges.tutor:
Actually, 'more choices' is a stronger starting point than you think - don't dismiss it! 

So push that a little further: choices about *what*, exactly? What kinds of things do you think people in developing countries might lack the freedom to choose or do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain the concept to them directly. I should not do this - it would violate the core rules. However, 'more choices' is actually a genuinely good intuition that I can build on using the Socratic method. I'll validate the insight and push them to develop it further rather than giving them the big picture myself.

exchanges.student: idk, like jobs, school, healthcare, voting maybe? tbh i still can’t tell how that turns into a planning argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good list. Now here's the key question: if a government planner accepts that development means expanding those freedoms - jobs, school, healthcare, voting - how does that *change* what they plan for, compared to, say, just trying to grow the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified concrete freedoms (jobs, education, healthcare, voting) which maps well onto Sen's capabilities framework. Now I should push them to make the connection between these freedoms and planning - that's the gap they identified. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question to bridge that gap without explaining it for them.

exchanges.student: maybe they’d care abt ppl’s actual lives, not just GDP, but i’m still fuzzy on the rights-based part fr. can u connect those for me?
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think. Here's a nudge: if access to healthcare or education is a *freedom* someone should have, what does it become when you frame it as something they are *entitled* to? What word comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they've connected 'development as freedom' to a focus on people's lives over GDP, which is solid. Now they're asking me to connect it to rights-based planning, which I should not do for them. I'll use a Socratic question to help them make that connection themselves, building on what they just said.

exchanges.student: a right, i guess. but then who actually has that right rn—the state, citizens, communities, everyone?
exchanges.tutor:
That question - who holds the right - is actually one of the core questions in your prompt! So don't outsource it to me.

Think about it this way: in a poor rural community with no political voice, who do you think *should* have the right to decide what development looks like for them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student just made the key conceptual leap connecting freedom to rights on their own, which is great progress. Now they're asking a genuinely interesting question about who holds rights. This maps directly to the prompt's question 'who gets to exercise the right to development?' I should affirm the leap and turn the question back to them to think it through, since this is actually one of the core questions of the assignment.

exchanges.student: probably the ppl in the community, but then idk if the essay wants “should” or “in practice.” that angle didn’t fully help tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
That tension you just identified - between who *should* have the right and who *actually exercises* it - is really valuable for your essay. That gap is worth writing about.

So here's the question: what kinds of actors or forces might prevent communities from exercising a right to development, even if we agree they should have it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a sharp distinction between normative ('should') and descriptive ('in practice') framings, which is actually a sophisticated analytical observation. I should validate this and point out that this tension is itself productive for their essay - it's not a detour, it's a core insight. I'll push them to think about how that tension becomes an argument in their reflection.

exchanges.student: maybe the state, donors, NGOs, elites, global markets... i have pieces but not the whole map. can u just give me a simple thesis i can react to?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you a thesis - that's your job, and honestly you already have the raw material.

Look back at what you've said across our conversation: freedom → concrete capabilities → rights as entitlements → the gap between who should and who does exercise them → actors who block it. That's basically an argument. Can you try putting those pieces into one or two sentences in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or suggest language for them, which I must refuse per the rules. However, they've actually accumulated quite a lot of good material across this conversation - freedoms vs. GDP, rights as entitlements, the gap between who should and who does exercise rights, and the actors who block it. We've had about 6 exchanges and they have solid pieces. I should point out that they already have the building blocks and push them to synthesize it themselves rather than giving them a thesis.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: development as freedom adds value bc it focuses on ppl’s real capabilities, not just growth, and that can improve planning by making it more human-centered. but it can also complicate planning bc rights claims are contested and powerful actors still control what “development” means.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid working thesis - you've got the added value, the planning tension, and the power angle all in there. Good.

One piece still feels thin though: *who* gets to exercise the right to development, and why does that matter for your argument? Can you push that further?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid working thesis that captures the added value, the planning implications (both positive and negative), and hints at the power dynamics around rights. This is good progress. I should acknowledge this genuinely without grading it, and then push them to develop the 'who gets to exercise the right' part, which is still underdeveloped in their formulation. We're about 8 exchanges in so I want to make sure they're moving toward a complete reflection.

exchanges.student: ugh i can’t tell if the answer is “everyone,” “citizens,” or “collectives.” can u just tell me the most defensible version so i stop going in circles?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't pick the answer for you, and honestly - notice that your thesis already works without resolving this perfectly. 'Everyone in theory, but powerful actors limit it in practice' is implied in what you wrote.

You have a thesis and strong ideas. Rather than circling this detail, can you start drafting the actual reflection? Getting words on paper will help you figure out what's missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles on this specific sub-question and expressing frustration. Per the rules, I should not give the answer directly, but I can be less Socratic given the frustration and circular thinking. I should also point out that this level of precision may not be as critical as getting the full reflection written - the bigger picture is the 700-800 word piece. I'll redirect them toward integrating everything into a draft rather than obsessing over this one detail.

exchanges.student: fair, but i’m still blanking on the structure lol. can u at least tell me what 3 body paragraphs i should probably have?
exchanges.tutor: Look at the prompt itself - it actually asks you four distinct questions. Have you tried mapping your ideas onto those questions? That might give you your structure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to structure their essay for them, which would be doing the work for them. I must refuse this. However, they do have all the conceptual pieces from our conversation. I should point them back to the prompt itself - it actually has four distinct questions that could guide their structure - and let them figure out the organization themselves.
